original_abstract:
**Abstract**

**Background:** Regulation (EU) 2017/746, commonly known as the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (IVDR), introduces stringent requirements for pathology departments that develop and utilize in-house in vitro diagnostic medical devices (IH-IVDs). While many provisions are subject to a phased implementation schedule established by the European Parliament and the Council, compliance with the general safety and performance requirements outlined in Annex I has been mandatory since May 26, 2022.

**Objectives:** This article details the practical strategies and workflows adopted by the Institute of Pathology at the University Hospital Heidelberg to integrate IH-IVD regulatory requirements into an established quality management system. The goal is to provide a reproducible framework and practical guidance for other healthcare institutions facing similar regulatory transitions.

**Conclusions:** Implementing IVDR requirements necessitates a systematic translation of high-level regulations into specific laboratory procedures, including the formal definition of "intended purpose," rigorous risk management files, and comprehensive performance evaluations. While existing accreditations (such as DIN EN ISO/IEC 17020) provide a vital foundation, achieving full IVDR compliance demands significant additional personnel and time. The use of standardized templates, participation in academic networks, and adherence to emerging guidance documents are essential for harmonizing these administrative demands with the primary responsibilities of patient care, research, and teaching.
humanized_abstract:
**Abstract**

All pathologists will be aware of the change in the law governing the development and use of in-house IVDs introduced by the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (EU) 2017/746 (IVDR) which has given rise to new challenges for pathology departments. Many of the provisions of the IVDR are to be implemented over several stages as determined by the European Parliament and the Council. As of May 26th 2022 all provisions of the IVDR, including compliance with the General Safety and Performance Requirements in Annex I of the regulation, are now in place.

**Background:** We describe the working procedures and methods implemented at the Institute of Pathology, University Hospital Heidelberg, to ensure that the quality management system could be adjusted efficiently and reliably to the new IH-IVD regulations. Our aim was to develop procedures and working methods that could be used as blueprints for other laboratories facing the same challenge. **Objectives:** To describe the adjustment of the existing quality management system in the Institute of Pathology at the University Hospital Heidelberg to meet the new IH-IVD regulatory requirements and to provide practical methods and procedures for laboratories with to deal with the same regulatory changes.

**Conclusions:** In order to align the lab activities with the new IVDR requirements, it will be necessary to convert the more general directives into practical procedures in the lab; this includes assigning the intended purpose of diagnostic reagents, including in risk management files, performing validations to assess the performance of analytical or diagnostic systems, as well as assessing the performance of reagents in any new analytical or diagnostic system in which they are used. This process will be carried out while the new certification (ISO 15189) continues and while personnel dedicate considerable time and effort to ensuring the entire process is executed in a way that the laboratory can remain open to patients, participating in research and with ongoing medical education. Moreover, the achievement of the new accreditation according to DIN EN ISO/IEC 17020 will be a minimum prerequisite for compliance with the new IVDR requirements. Instead, implementing the new procedures in an effective manner and obtaining the required certificate will require considerable extra personnel and time. In order to align these administration tasks in an optimal manner to the main working activities of physicians – namely patient care, research and medical education – it will be crucial to use standardized procedures and forms and also to participate in relevant continuing medical education activities in order to also have an overview of the updated publications or directives.
